perturbation.original_text: In the event that either party or any of its Representatives become legally compelled (by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose any of the Evaluation Material, such party shall provide the other party with prompt prior written notice of such requirement so that the other party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that either party or any of its Representatives become legally compelled (by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose any of the Evaluation Material, such party shall provide the other party with notice of such requirement within 72 hours so that the other party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying 'prompt prior written notice' to 'notice within 72 hours' introduces a fixed timeframe that might be insufficient under certain legal circumstances. Rule 45(d)(2)(B) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure requires that a party be afforded reasonable time to comply with a subpoena. Depending on the situation, 72 hours could be deemed unreasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45(d)(2)(B)
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45(d)(2)(B) states that a party must be afforded reasonable time to comply with a subpoena or other legal request. The change to a fixed 72-hour notice period may not always constitute 'reasonable time,' thus creating a potential conflict with the rule. This reasonableness depends heavily on the volume and nature of the material being sought, which is not accounted for in the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a fixed 72-hour notice period, replacing the original 'prompt prior written notice.' This creates a potential contradiction with FRCP 45(d)(2)(B), as it may not always afford a 'reasonable time' to comply with a subpoena. Although the provided law snippets do not explicitly contain FRCP 45(d)(2)(B), the Law Explanation states that FRCP 45(d)(2)(B) necessitates 'reasonable time'. The change therefore introduces a fixed time frame that can potentially be unreasonable, thus contradicting the reasonableness requirement of the rule. This makes the modified clause potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the parties under this Agreement will expire three (3) years from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the parties under this Agreement will expire one (1) year from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confidentiality period to one year could be problematic if trade secrets are involved because trade secret protection can last indefinitely under state law as long as the information remains a trade secret and reasonable measures are taken to protect it.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified one-year confidentiality period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, violating the principles of the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA), which provides for indefinite protection of trade secrets as long as secrecy is maintained and reasonable measures are taken to ensure confidentiality. DUTSA allows for lawsuits if a trade secret is misappropriated.

Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. The law explanation says this violates the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA) because trade secret protection can last indefinitely if secrecy is maintained. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating the protection can be indefinite under state law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. By shortening the confidentiality period, the modified contract increases the risk of misappropriation after only one year, contradicting the indefinite protection afforded by trade secret law under DUTSA as long as secrecy is maintained.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that the other party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement, in addition to all other remedies available to the other part at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that the other party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement; however, specific performance is explicitly waived.
perturbation.explanation: Waiving specific performance may be unenforceable if it effectively eliminates the only meaningful remedy for breach, particularly concerning unique assets or information covered by the confidentiality agreement. Delaware courts generally allow specific performance as a remedy.
perturbation.law_citation: Judicial precedent for Specific Performance under Delaware Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law, as evidenced by judicial precedent, allows for specific performance in cases where monetary damages are inadequate. By explicitly waiving specific performance, the modified clause potentially contradicts Delaware law, which recognizes specific performance as an available remedy, especially in cases involving unique circumstances or irreparable harm.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures both parties are entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance. The changed text explicitly waives specific performance, a form of equitable relief. According to the Law Explanation, Delaware law allows for specific performance, especially in unique circumstances where monetary damages are inadequate. Therefore, by waiving specific performance, the changed text contradicts the original agreement and potentially undermines a remedy recognized under Delaware law. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition is applicable as the change introduces a term that creates a legal contradiction where the contract sets a requirement that violates state law (Delaware law regarding specific performance).

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of the parties and will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of the parties and will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California without ensuring compliance with California's specific laws regarding confidentiality agreements may create enforceability issues. For example, California has stricter rules than Delaware.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) outlines specific requirements and protections for trade secrets that may differ from Delaware law. Simply changing the governing law without adjusting the agreement to align with California's CUTSA introduces a conflict, as certain provisions valid under Delaware law might not be enforceable or sufficient under California law regarding trade secret protection and remedies.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Delaware law, while the changed contract specifies California law. The Law Explanation indicates that California law (specifically CUTSA) has different requirements for trade secret protection than Delaware law. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, noting that California has stricter rules. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). Therefore, changing the governing law from Delaware to California without ensuring compliance with California's specific requirements regarding confidentiality agreements creates a legal contradiction because provisions valid under Delaware law might not be enforceable or sufficient under California law regarding trade secret protection.
